videos.title: I Spent 90 Days in Dar Es Salaam: Here's What I Learned!
videos.description:
Dar Es Salaam is Tanzania's main economic hub. It is known to be a business city, so what is it like to live there as a tourist? For me, it was the city that changed everything. From the chaos of Kariakoo to sunsets at The Slipway, Dar es Salaam taught me things I didn’t expect about people, patience, and peace. In this Dar es Salaam video, I share my personal experience living in Tanzania’s largest city: the beaches, markets, and everyday life that taught me valuable lessons about travel and perspective. If you’ve ever wondered what it’s really like to explore Dar, this video gives you an honest, local-inspired look.

Check out My Services here: https://bit.ly/yaramelservices

Watch my Arusha video: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6eoWgmH-BM4

___________________________________________________________________



Instagram: https://instagram.com/_yaramel​

For Travel partnerships: ymas (dot) brands (at) gmail (dot) com



From Mozambique to Tanzania!

#DaresSalaam  #DarEsSalaamTanzania #TravelVlog #EastAfrica

videos.transcript: This is Daris Salam, a city of 7 million people with a coastline that hugs the Indian Ocean and hosts pockets of paradise. >> This is definitely one of the best places I've ever seen. The water is pretty clean and I feel like jumping in. >> Dar is the commercial capital of Tanzania, the heartbeat of the economy. Haraku market alone is said to move $10 million every week. That's more than some towns in East Africa move in a year. I've been traveling up from Mozambi to East Africa and Tanzania is my fourth country on the journey. After my first stop in Arouchia, Dar is my second city in the country and I must say it's chaotic, humid. >> I haven't sweat this much in a while. >> It's fast and slow at the same time. >> How is it that when I go to No, why is it that it doesn't make sense? >> That's what I thought when I first arrived. I had a checklist of non-negotiables to enjoy the city. And it didn't take long for me to realize that my checklist never stood a chance. Dar isn't like Nairobi or Kala or Kgali. It doesn't want to be. It doesn't need to. Dar is a city that works well for its people and that's enough. >> So people have been able to move to Dar Salam in search of opportunities for trade. Every place in Dar Salam, it's a city within a city. That's what makes Dar Salam one major commercial hub in Tanzania. >> For 90 days, I tried to fit in and I couldn't. But I learned six crucial lessons about Daris Salam and that's what I'll be sharing with you in this video. This video is brought to you by the clients that I work with online creators and entrepreneurs building strong brands for their businesses. Check out the link below or in the description box for my services deck. Enjoy the video. Arisalam wasn't built for tourists and that changes everything. Unlike Arouchia or Zanzibar, Dar isn't trying to charm tourists. This is a city that functions for business, trade, and for its people. I finally understand why I've rarely seen videos about tourism in Dar Salamon. It is very much a business city. I knew Masaki was the place to be as a tourist, but I still didn't understand why all the nice spots had to be concentrated there. After I learned lesson number two, the doubt was cleared. But going back to the city not being built for tourists, one aspect that stood out for me is the customer service. I had some not so pleasant experiences because for the first time as a customer, I was not the main character. I was just another customer. When I got to the hair salon, there were about 15 hairdressers scrolling on Tik Tok and none of them seemed excited to see me. I got to the massage place and the massage bed was a normal bed with a pillow. I don't need to get a massage that's going to injure my neck. And then she went to the other room and there were two massage beds there. I guess they didn't want to use it or something. She put the AC on. It was freezing and I told her, "It's it's a bit cold. Can you turn it off?" and she's like, "Oh, I really need the AC." Mid massage. She left for 10 minutes twice. So, I was just laying there cold. That was quite an experience. I have a feeling that at local businesses, customer service is better experienced by locals themselves or at least by sui speakers. And we will talk more about the language barrier because that alone was a big lesson. I still feel like any city that deliberately chooses not to cater to tourists is missing out on a lot of money. But as I later came to find out, money is not what drives Tanzanians. At least in Daris Salam. They seem to have other priorities that come before money. And you know what? Good for them. So why is it that all the hot spots or places that cater to tourists are all concentrated in the peninsula along Oyster Bay and Masaki? That's because their geography is shaped by colonial urban divisions. There's a reason why the tourist areas feel so different. Masaki and Oyster Bay were originally for Europeans. Upanga for Indians, Karyaku for Indians and Africans, Central Dar for Africans. During the Germans, Dar Salam was divided into zones and racial lines. The British just continued division of Dar Salam into zones through racial lines. Osta Bay became a major British administrative area. These divides still live on in how the city looks, feels, and even costs. This area has a completely different look and feel from what I'm used to in central Dar from the luxurious apartments, the nicer roads that are pretty much empty. This is the type of neighborhood you get when you pay a premium price in Masaki. Basically, the city's most conventionally beautiful areas were once European zones. And unfortunately, that legacy still shows. So, let's explore Masaki and see why is it so hyped amongst locals and tourists alike. We're about to enter one of the most beautiful places in Dar Salam, which is the Sleepway. >> Yeah. [Music] >> Fun fact, guys. I once dreamed about a place exactly like this. Little did I know that that place existed in Dar Salam. Honestly, this was the most beautiful hangout space I've ever been to in East Africa. No wonder why I dreamed of it before. The hotel design is stunning along with curated shops, restaurants, interesting corners, and an extensive seafront prominent. It's easy to be here for hours and hours without getting bored. [Music] I have no words to describe this place. Besides being such a beautiful infrastructure right in front of the water, the water is pretty clean and I feel like jumping in. Would it be bad for me to wear a bikini and jump in? There's like all these people. But this is insane. Like Dar Salam, what? Hands down the most beautiful place in the city. And it was so well taught, so well done, and so pleasing. Especially today on a nice sunny day. The sun is about to set, and there's nowhere else I'd rather be in Dar Salam. I just want to dive. Let's go. [Music] [Music] I chose to live in central Daris Salam for a more local experience. And while I got that, I also got struggle. That's when I learned living in Dar central means living real and a bit hard. I basically paid to be uncomfortable, but I gained a lot of perspective. And by Dar central, I mean Upanga and Karyaku. I cross Moro road almost every day, and every time I admire the dedicated BRT lanes. The buses don't get stuck in traffic, which helps shorten the journey between the city and outer neighborhoods, saving people hours every week. Moro Road is my favorite on Sundays, obviously, because you can appreciate the beauty of the road. There aren't much cars, not much traffic, and the sun at 5:00 p.m. sets beautifully right at the end of the world. Look. [Music] [Music] This is the highly rated level eight rooftop. A great place to come and have drinks whilst enjoying amazing views of Dar Salam. [Music] This is City Mall, a place that has been a one-stop shop for me to pick up things, run errands. I do my grocery shopping here. The other day, I was talking to the managing director, and he told me that all of the stores here are locally owned. So, this is a very big economic hub for the neighborhood. It's a fourstory building and they have a cinema right at the top. They also have a really good gym. It is Sunday morning and it's upper body day for me. >> Guys, I'm here with my trainer. What's your name? >> Aman. >> And I kid you not, some exercises I've been doing wrong for the past 10 years. And it took Atman to like help me fix my my form. >> You're most welcome. The city mall >> asante [Music] is the first thing I was doing wrong for the past 10 years. I used to do this but I learned with atma that I'm straining my neck. So 10 years later, I'm doing it the right way. So my whole back is straight. >> Better late than never. Sundays are so calm. Absolutely my favorite. Living here also allowed me to gain a lot of perspective from the main trading hub in the city, Karyaku Market. Known as the beating heart of Daris Salam's economy, this is where goods from across Tanzania and beyond change hands every day. It is the place that allegedly moves millions of dollars worth of trade weekly. A market so vast and alive that it feels like an entire city within a city. Everyone goes to Karaako if they need something to to purchase. So it's the major hub in trade not only in Dar Salam but today in the whole of Tanzania. After the busy streets of Dar central crossing the Tanzanite Bridge feels like entering a different world. Poco Beach is where the city slows down. It's a place to relax, play sports, eat or swim in the warm Indian Ocean. So this is the famous Cocoa Beach. [Music] One can definitely fulfill all of their swimming needs right here at Cocoa Beach. That's a luxury to have in such a big city like Tis Salam. I decided to come and have a seat at Wuvi, which is one of the most stunning beach clubs I've ever seen. It's one of the hot spots here in Cocoa Beach. Absolutely worth it. Their drinks menu had some interesting names. I decided to order the samura mashell. Of course, I have to be patriotic. [Music] [Music] The best part about living in central Dardo is eating like a local and that sealed my next lesson. Dar Salam probably has the best food in East Africa in my humble opinion. And I say this because I come from a country where we season our food a lot and in Dar I finally felt that again. The seasoning of the food felt familiar yet distinctive enough to become memorable in my mind and in my taste buds. I'm now heading to the restaurant that I've enjoyed the most throughout my stay in Dis Salam. It has kept me extremely wellfed. 80% of the time I am the only customer just like today. And I feel so bad cuz I feel like I'm the only one enjoying such delicious food. [Music] Starting off with a classic starter samosa plate. Now the start of the show, the best chicken sella. [Music] >> It's steak o'clock in my household. And even though I could have ordered delivery, I decided I'll just take you guys to the spot that makes some of the best steak in Dar Salam. I'm here with the founder now, Jamil. What's the secret? What makes the steak so juicy and tender? >> Yas, man. Magic hands. >> No, we imported >> from where? >> Zambia. >> So that's why it has a special taste. Okay, now you know [Music] [Applause] [Music] >> any street food corner with humble facilities and plastic chairs can become the best gastronomic experience you've ever had. This lady stationed on the road close to my building made me feel very lucky to eat her warm mandazi in many mornings. Dar isn't just made of street restaurants. It also has some amazing high-end establishments. >> I did a thorough research and I think I found what is the most beautiful restaurant in Dar Salam. [Music] This place is beautiful. So many seating areas, so many different environments. Absolutely great work by the interior design team. [Applause] [Music] The food is what I'll miss the most about Dis Salam. Tanzanians season their food like it's a love language, just like Muslim vegans do, to be honest. But keep in mind that the best restaurants with the tastiest food do not show off. You have to dig into the local areas to find them. Now, let's address the elephant in the room, the language barrier. And right off the bat, I can say without suili, you'll never truly experience the city and its people. You can survive in English, but you'll never really connect. Most people don't care about speaking English here. And it's not out of rudeness. It's just because they don't need it for survival. And so runs deep. When it comes to numbers, it's my fault because I never took the time to sit down and memorize the numbers. In Kenya, the prices go by the hundreds and dozens. And here, the prices go by the thousands. So is the only thing I know. >> And say it again. >> Just tell me how to say it. Yeah. Yeah. You keep on going. You're saying you're saying Yes. Yes. It's not easy. Come on baby guys. I say watch. So, how was my Tanzanian? >> So bad. So bad. Let me tell you a Tanzanian. You sound like a rich spoiled kid. >> Josh, you're such a hater. >> I'm not hate. I'm just saying facts. You're like Abby Chums. You really sound so spoiled. You sound like you come from a rich family, but >> I'm just a girl. The language is key to approaching people, asking for prices, direction, everything. Especially understanding jokes, which I never did here. This was another humbling lesson. And speaking of humbling, lesson number six is exactly that. If you're not patient, Dar will humble you. Here's the caveat. Under all the chaos, Dar moves slowly, not in speed, but in attitude. People don't rush, so systems don't work as fast. Nothing will go your way unless you relax. which very much reminds me of Maputo, my hometown. I wonder if it's something to do with coastal cities. The nervous systems are overly regulated, so there's no rush to do anything. The bad news is you can't get around it. The good news is if you stay here long enough, you stop judging and you start adapting. In Dar Salam, memories are defined by people, not landmarks. The people make it. And if you don't connect with the locals, you just won't get it. I of course ended up connecting with locals that helped me understand Dar better, enjoy the city, and most importantly learned these lessons that I'll take with me forever. I'm grateful for Tanzania, especially Dar Salam, for completely pushing me out of my comfort zone. It took a while, but I ended up falling in love with the city for what it is and not for what I wanted it to be. So, I'll definitely be back Dar and I'll see you guys on the next

videos.title: First time in TANZANIA! | is Dar es Salaam worth visiting?
videos.description:
Dar es Salaam, Tanzania Travel Vlog. My first time traveling to Africa! First impression of Dar Es Salaam, Tanzania's biggest city, as solo female traveler! In this Dar es Salaam travel vlog I show top attractions and best places to see in Dar es Salaam, share my thought on whether Dar es Salaam is a safe city for tourist, where to stay & what to know before visiting. 

Get MY BOOK 'I lived in Shanghai' on Amazon! https://amzn.to/3pLoeQ4 

Instagram @bykajaa https://www.instagram.com/bykajaa/
TikTok @bykajaa https://www.tiktok.com/@bykajaa?lang=en

0:00 My first time traveling Tanzania & Africa! (why I'm terrified?) 
1:30 Exploring Masaki & Slipway (first impression of Dar es Salaam) 
3:20 Is Dar es Salaam safe for tourists? 
5:40 Coco beach, Karibu & Mambo (being a tourist in Tanzania is difficult?)
7:10 How to get around Dar es Salaam? (The Slow Leopard hostel)
8:25 What to do in Dar es Salaam? (Botanical Garden, St. Joseph Cathedral) 11:00 Kariakoo Market (best places to see in Dar es Salaam?)
13:30 Tinga Tinga Arts Cooperative Society
15:10 Where does the name 'Tanzania' come from?
15:45 Is Dar es Salaam worth visiting? (what's next?)

MUSIC from https://www.epidemicsound.com/referral/ckfcvu/

Contact me at bykaja @ outlook.com 
https://www.kajakubicka.com/
Facebook https://www.facebook.com/bykajaa/

#tanzania #daressalaam #africa
